turns.content: So I was skimming the past exam, and they've got a Paxos question. Ugh. Can you remind me how the prepare/accept phases work?

Sure! In Paxos, the prepare phase involves a proposer sending a 'prepare' request with a proposal number to a majority of acceptors. If an acceptor receives a higher proposal number than any it has seen before, it responds with a 'promise' to not accept lower numbers and may include the highest-numbered proposal it has accepted. Then, in the accept phase, the proposer sends an 'accept' request for a value, which is accepted by the acceptors if they have promised not to accept lower numbers. This is the basic flow!
Right, but how does that actually handle failures? Like, what if a proposer crashes after sending prepares?

If a proposer crashes after sending prepares, other proposers can take over. As long as a majority of acceptors have made promises and recorded the accepted values, another proposer can still drive the consensus forward. They can use the highest accepted value from the responses they get back to make a new proposal, ensuring that the system can continue to make progress even after failures.
That makes sense. But seriously, the whole thing feels way more complicated than Raft. What do you think?

I get that! Raft simplifies things with its leader-based approach, where the leader manages the log replication and handles conflicts directly. Paxos can feel like a maze because of the multiple roles and phases, but it’s still a powerful method. It's more about proving correctness in theory, while Raft is about practical implementation.
Lol, totally. The textbook calls Paxos 'elegant,' but have they tried coding it? Raft is just way easier to grasp.

Exactly! Raft's structure—where a single leader coordinates everything—makes it easier to implement and reason about. Paxos has its elegance in theory, but the complexity can lead to confusion in real implementations. Plus, it’s all about how you define success criteria, which can make a big difference in practice.
Okay, so if I want to convince myself Paxos isn't just a theoretical exercise, what should I focus on?

Focus on the real-world scenarios where Paxos is applied, like in Google Chubby or other distributed systems. Understanding how it resolves conflicts and achieves consensus in those cases can help you appreciate its practical side. Implementing a basic version, even if it’s simplified, can also give you a better grasp of how it operates.
Got it. I’ll definitely look into the implementations. By the way, what about the safety and liveness properties? I keep mixing those up.

Safety ensures that once a value is chosen, it can't be changed—like once a consensus is reached, that decision holds. Liveness, on the other hand, guarantees that the system will eventually make progress and reach a decision. In Paxos, these properties are intertwined, but it's crucial to remember that safety is about consistency, while liveness is about availability.
Okay, safety is like not rolling back decisions, and liveness is about keeping the system moving. I think I can remember that. Thanks!
Exactly! Just keep practicing those definitions, and you’ll nail it. If you can sketch out the scenarios where each property might fail, that'll solidify your understanding even more.
I’ll do that. Oh, and can you send me some pseudocode for Paxos one last time? I want to compare it side-by-side with Raft.

Sure! Here’s a basic outline for Paxos: 
1. Propose with a number. 
2. Receive promises from acceptors. 
3. If enough promises, send accept requests for a value. 
4. Collect responses to determine if a value is accepted. 

In Raft, it’s much more straightforward with the leader sending log entries to followers and handling replication and conflict resolution. This side-by-side will really help clarify the differences!
Awesome, thanks! I’ll make sure to compare the two closely. I'm feeling better about Paxos now.
Great to hear! Remember, the more you work through the nuances, the easier it’ll become. Good luck with your study, and feel free to reach out if you have more questions!
